The shift signals Google's commitment to distributing development capability downward while centralizing app discovery through its own AI systems.
items.original_title: Google’s AI Studio now lets anyone build Android apps in minutes

items.headline: Google adds conversational search to Gmail, limits rollout to premium tiers
items.body:
Google has expanded its AI capabilities in Gmail with a new conversational search feature called Gmail Live, powered by its Gemini model. Rather than typing keywords into a search box, users can now ask natural-language questions about their inbox contents—locating flight details, appointment times, hotel confirmations, or other buried information through dialogue with an AI assistant. The feature launches this summer initially for Google AI Ultra subscribers, with plans to extend to AI Pro and Plus tiers.

## How Gmail Live Works

Gmail Live handles multi-turn conversations, follow-up questions, and topic shifts without explicit reframing. In a demonstration to reporters, Google's product lead Devanshi Bhandari showed the tool retrieving granular details like hotel room numbers and inferring context about people mentioned across multiple emails. The system distinguishes between similar terms—"field trip" versus "trip"—and pulls relevant messages without requiring exact search terms. Voice input is also supported, mirroring the interaction model of standalone chatbots like Gemini or ChatGPT.

The feature does not replace traditional Gmail search; it operates as an optional alternative. Google is simultaneously rolling out other inbox updates, including AI-drafted responses, instant file access, and task management capabilities. The broader AI Inbox experience, which provides a consolidated view of actionable items, is expanding beyond Ultra subscribers to reach Pro and Plus tier users.

## Google's Pragmatic Approach to AI Integration

Google appears to have internalized lessons from its Google Photos rollback, where an AI-only search redesign triggered widespread user complaints and forced the company to make the feature optional. That experience demonstrates enterprise and consumer reluctance toward mandatory AI-first interfaces, even when functional improvements exist.

For Gmail, the decision to position conversational search as additive rather than replacive suggests Google is calibrating its strategy around user choice. This matters for enterprise adoption: organizations evaluating AI-integrated products increasingly expect backward compatibility and feature parity with existing workflows.

The tiered rollout also signals a shift in Google's AI monetization: advanced AI features are now differentiators between subscription tiers, similar to how compute capacity drives pricing in cloud platforms. For enterprises, this means evaluating not just whether conversational search solves real problems—finding information in large inboxes is genuinely frequent—but whether the premium tier cost justifies adoption versus existing search workflows.
items.original_title: Google’s AI now lets you talk to your Gmail inbox

items.headline: Google Omni Merges Text, Audio, Video in Single Generative Model
items.body:
Google announced Gemini Omni at its I/O developer conference, a family of multimodal models that consolidate text, image, audio, and video processing into a unified system. Rather than stitching inputs together, Omni reasons across all modalities simultaneously to generate consistent outputs. The company positioned this as progress toward its three-year goal of building a single neural network capable of understanding and creating content in any format.

## Initial Release and Capabilities

The first model, Gemini Omni Flash, rolls out today to the Gemini app, YouTube Shorts, and Google's AI creative studio Flow. Flash generates up to 10 seconds of video—a choice driven by consumer accessibility rather than technical limitation. Google says longer durations are in development. The model demonstrates reasoning about physics, culture, history, and science: when given a prompt like "a claymation explainer of protein folding," it produced stop-motion video with synchronized voiceover.

Flash also handles photo editing via plain text commands and enables users to create videos featuring their own digital avatars. Google requires identity verification—users record themselves speaking numbers—before generating avatar content, a measure intended to prevent deepfake misuse. All generated videos receive Google's SynthID digital watermark for verification. An enterprise-grade Omni Pro model is planned for later release but no timeline has been announced.

## Enterprise Implications and Workflow Integration

Google will release Omni via API in coming weeks, targeting creators and advertisers. A unified multimodal workflow could streamline production pipelines: content creators can generate videos directly from text and image inputs without moving between specialized tools. Google highlighted text-rendering accuracy as a selling point for advertising applications, where product placement and messaging must be precise.

The broader signal is clear: generative AI is shifting from single-modality to end-to-end content creation. Startups like Luma AI are pursuing similar unified models for ad campaigns, suggesting this is becoming table stakes for creative tools. Google's execution advantage lies in scale and existing distribution channels. For enterprise users, this means evaluating whether Omni's integrated approach reduces production friction enough to warrant adoption over specialized tools—particularly as Pro capabilities mature and API access expands. The decisive factor will be whether the quality-to-setup ratio justifies workflow migration.
items.original_title: Google’s Gemini Omni turns images, audio, and text into video — and that’s just the start
